title: Europe's resistance to AC is driving it insane
gist: Smith argues Europe's elite-driven resistance to air conditioning, rooted in degrowth ideology and cultural conservatism, is killing roughly 175,000 people a year for negligible climate benefit, and treats it as a case study in how societies impoverish themselves by rejecting superior foreign technology.

insights.claim: Heat kills an estimated 175,000 people a year across Europe, a death rate of about 23.5 per 100,000, nearly twice the US firearm death rate of about 13.7 per 100,000.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of a preventable mortality problem that creates large latent demand for cooling technology across a 745M-person market.
insights.quote: the death rate from heat in Europe is almost twice the death rate from guns in America
insights.locator: So the death rate from heat in Europe

insights.claim: No data source puts European (or UK) AC penetration above roughly 20%, meaning about 80% of European households lack air conditioning.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: An ~80% un-penetrated market is a structurally large addressable opportunity for HVAC, mini-split, and heat-pump makers if cultural resistance breaks.
insights.quote: nobody puts AC usage in Europe (or the UK) at more than around 20%
insights.locator: Data sources differ, but nobody puts

insights.claim: Barreca et al. (2016) found US heat deaths fell about 75% after 1960 and that residential AC diffusion explains essentially the entire decline in hot-day fatalities; applied to Europe this implies up to ~100,000 lives could be saved annually by full AC adoption.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Provides a hard causal estimate linking AC rollout to mortality reduction, strengthening the eventual policy and demand case for European AC.
insights.quote: the diffusion of residential air conditioning explains essentially the entire decline in hot day–related fatalities
insights.locator: Barreca et al. (2016) find that heat deaths

insights.claim: Cooling devices like AC already account for about 10% of global electricity consumption, with most of that power still from fossil fuels.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A 10%-and-rising share of global electricity going to cooling is a durable structural driver of power demand and a tailwind for generation/grid capacity.
insights.quote: cooling devices like ACs account for about 10% of global electricity consumption

insights.claim: 8 out of 10 people in France want their country to install AC in houses, schools, and public transit, even as European elites actively campaign against it, revealing a sharp elite-vs-public split on cooling.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Strong popular demand against elite obstruction signals the policy/cultural barrier is the binding constraint, not consumer desire, so a political shift could unlock the market fast.
insights.quote: 8 out of 10 people in France — the country hardest hit by the most recent heat wave — want their country to install AC
insights.locator: Regular Europeans are hitting the breaking point.

insights.claim: European institutions actively suppress AC: officials spread false claims that portable units or AC cannot beat heat waves, and the European Commission cut AC for lower-floor staff while commissioners on upper floors kept theirs.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Illustrates institutional, not just consumer, resistance, the kind of top-down barrier that slows technology penetration regardless of demand.
insights.quote: forced shut down of air cooling system from floor 1 to 7 for the rest of the day
insights.locator: Staff working at the Berlaymont building received a text

insights.claim: Europe accounts for only about 13% of global fossil-fuel carbon emissions, so the climate benefit of region-wide AC installation is minimal relative to the tens of thousands of lives it would save annually.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The cost-benefit math (negligible emissions vs. huge mortality) argues the anti-AC stance is ideological, implying it is reversible under political pressure.
insights.quote: Europe is responsible for only about 13% of global carbon emissions from fossil fuel use
insights.locator: Right now, Europe is responsible for only

insights.claim: Degrowth ideology frames climate change as a problem of personal overconsumption to be solved by austere self-restraint rather than by deploying green energy, a framing Smith calls foolish because it causes suffering without meaningfully curbing emissions.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies degrowth as a policy headwind to energy-demand growth and consumer tech adoption in Europe, distinct from the build-green-energy approach.
insights.quote: Degrowth frames climate change as a problem of personal overconsumption and extravagance to be curbed by austere self-restraint
insights.locator: This is immediately recognizable as the poisonous ideology of degrowth.

insights.claim: European regulations directly block AC, including carbon-neutral mandates for new buildings, permit requirements, heritage-preservation rules, and outright bans, with fines in Italy ranging up to EUR43,000.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Regulatory barriers are concrete and quantified; tracking their repeal would be the leading indicator that the European cooling market is opening.
insights.quote: The fines can range up to €43,000, though most cases are settled out of court by a removal of the unit.

insights.claim: Cultural conservatism, the desire to protect traditional culture from foreign technology, is a major underlying reason some societies reject superior technologies, with Japan's wakon-yosai (Japanese soul, Western technology) as the model for adopting tech without losing identity.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Offers a predictive heuristic: societies that treat a technology as a foreign cultural threat will under-adopt it regardless of its utility.
insights.quote: Cultural conservatism might be a big part of why some cultures eagerly adopt new and superior technologies, while others turn up their noses and refuse.
insights.locator: But I think part of it is simple cultural conservatism

insights.claim: Lee Kuan Yew credited air conditioning as one of the signal inventions of history, saying it made development possible in the tropics and was key to Singapore's public-sector efficiency and wealth.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Reframes AC from a comfort good to a productivity-and-development enabler, supporting the thesis that cooling adoption raises economic output.
insights.quote: It changed the nature of civilization by making development possible in the tropics.
insights.locator: That city-state's legendary founder, Lee Kuan Yew

insights.claim: Europe's rejection of AC is making it a more impoverished, shabbier civilization relative to America despite its beautiful old cities and low crime, framing tech-resistance as a driver of relative economic decline.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Connects technology adoption directly to long-run living-standards and the widening US-Europe prosperity gap, a macro theme for relative-growth positioning.
insights.quote: the resistance to AC technology is making Europe a more impoverished civilization
insights.locator: Whatever the reason, the resistance to AC technology
